steps.content: A curious student wants to learn about space exploration and chat about recent missions.
Role: curious student. Expertise: beginner. Domain: science and space. Goals: learn new things; get clear explanations. Personality: expressive and enthusiastic.
chit_chat/new_topic
steps.hint: Generate a user message that introduces a new topic or asks a fresh open-ended question.

steps.content: Hey! I was just reading about the James Webb Space Telescope. What's the most exciting thing it has discovered so far?

The James Webb Space Telescope has made some truly mind-blowing discoveries! One of the most exciting is the detection of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere of exoplanet WASP-39b — it was the first clear evidence of CO2 in an exoplanet's atmosphere. Webb has also captured incredibly detailed images of galaxies that formed just a few hundred million years after the Big Bang, giving us our first clear look at the very early universe. Which aspect are you most curious about — exoplanets, early galaxies, or something else?
chit_chat/follow_up
steps.hint: Generate a user message that naturally follows up on what was just discussed.

steps.content: The exoplanet stuff sounds amazing! Could Webb actually find signs of life on another planet someday?

It's possible, though finding definitive signs of life is incredibly difficult. Webb can analyze the chemical composition of exoplanet atmospheres by measuring which wavelengths of starlight get absorbed as a planet transits its star. Scientists call certain combinations of gases — like oxygen, methane, or nitrous oxide together — 'biosignatures,' because they strongly suggest biological processes. Webb is sensitive enough to detect some of these. It won't be a smoking gun, but a promising detection would be a huge moment for science.
chit_chat/termination
steps.hint: Generate a user message that naturally closes the conversation.

steps.content: This has been so cool to learn about. Thanks for explaining it so clearly!
You're very welcome! Space is endlessly fascinating — Webb is just getting started. If you ever want to dive deeper into any of these topics, just ask. Happy exploring!
A busy professional needs a quick recommendation for a book to read during their commute.
Role: busy professional. Expertise: intermediate. Domain: productivity and technology. Goals: get quick answers; save time. Style: prefers concise, direct answers; no small talk.
steps.style_override: prefers concise, direct answers; no small talk

steps.content: chit_chat/new_topic
steps.hint: Generate a user message that introduces a new topic or asks a fresh open-ended question.

steps.content: I have a 30-minute commute and want to start a new audiobook. I liked Atomic Habits. Any similar recommendations?

If you liked Atomic Habits, you'd probably enjoy 'The Power of Habit' by Charles Duhigg — it covers the science behind habits in more depth. 'Deep Work' by Cal Newport is excellent if you want to improve focus and productivity. For something slightly different but equally practical, 'Four Thousand Weeks' by Oliver Burkeman challenges how we think about time management. All three work great as audiobooks.
chit_chat/clarification
steps.hint: Generate a user message asking for clarification or more detail.

steps.content: Between Deep Work and Four Thousand Weeks, which one is more actionable day-to-day?

Deep Work is more immediately actionable — it gives you concrete strategies for structuring focused work sessions and eliminating distractions. Four Thousand Weeks is more philosophical; it reframes your relationship with time but the 'actions' are more about mindset shifts than daily tactics. If you want something you can apply starting this week, go with Deep Work.
chit_chat/termination
steps.hint: Generate a user message that naturally closes the conversation.

steps.content: Perfect, I'll start with Deep Work. Thanks!
Great choice! Enjoy the commute.
A retiree wants to chat about their recent trip to Italy and get restaurant recommendations.
Role: retired hobbyist. Expertise: beginner. Domain: travel and food. Goals: share stories; get recommendations. Personality: expressive and enthusiastic, cooperative and accommodating.
chit_chat/share_opinion
steps.hint: Generate a user message where the user shares a personal view or experience.

steps.content: We just got back from two weeks in Italy and I have to say, the food in Bologna was absolutely extraordinary. Have you heard much about Bolognese cuisine?

Bologna is called 'La Grassa' — The Fat One — for good reason! It's considered the culinary capital of Italy. The ragù Bolognese there is nothing like what most people outside Italy have eaten — it's slow-cooked with very little tomato, rich with meat and a splash of milk. Mortadella, the original inspiration for bologna sausage, is also from there. Did you have any particularly memorable meals?
chit_chat/follow_up
steps.hint: Generate a user message that naturally follows up on what was just discussed.

steps.content: We had tagliatelle al ragù at a tiny place near the Piazza Maggiore. I want to try to recreate it at home. Any tips?

The key is patience and restraint. Use a mix of beef and pork, cook the meat slowly in a soffritto of onion, celery, and carrot until it's deeply caramelized — about 45 minutes before you add any liquid. Add just a tablespoon or two of tomato paste, a splash of whole milk or cream, and then a cup of white wine. Let it simmer on the lowest heat for at least two hours, stirring occasionally. Serve on fresh egg tagliatelle, not spaghetti — that's actually prohibited by Bologna's chamber of commerce!
chit_chat/termination
steps.hint: Generate a user message that naturally closes the conversation.

steps.content: I love that detail about the spaghetti prohibition! I'm going to try this recipe this weekend. Thank you so much.
Enjoy the cooking — and the memories from the trip! Buona fortuna!
